name: pygraphviz

metadata.name: pygraphviz
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules

documentation:
# PyGraphviz

```{image} https://github.com/pygraphviz/pygraphviz/workflows/test/badge.svg?branch=main
:target: https://github.com/pygraphviz/pygraphviz/actions?query=workflow%3Atest+branch%3Amain
```

```{image} https://codecov.io/gh/pygraphviz/pygraphviz/branch/main/graph/badge.svg
:target: https://app.codecov.io/gh/pygraphviz/pygraphviz/branch/main
```

PyGraphviz is a Python interface to the Graphviz graph layout and
visualization package.
With PyGraphviz you can create, edit, read, write, and draw graphs using
Python to access the Graphviz graph data structure and layout algorithms.
PyGraphviz provides a similar programming interface to NetworkX
(<https://networkx.org>).

- **Website (including documentation):** <https://pygraphviz.github.io>
- **Mailing list:** <https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/pygraphviz-discuss>
- **Source:** <https://github.com/pygraphviz/pygraphviz>
- **Bug reports:** <https://github.com/pygraphviz/pygraphviz/issues>

## Simple example

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_node("a")
>>> G.add_edge("b", "c")
>>> print(G)
strict graph "" {
        a;
        b -- c;
}
```

## Install

PyGraphviz requires Graphviz.
Please see `INSTALL.txt` for details.

## License

Released under the 3-Clause BSD license (see `LICENSE`):

```
Copyright (C) 2006-2024 PyGraphviz Developers
Aric Hagberg <aric.hagberg@gmail.gov>
Dan Schult <dschult@colgate.edu>
Manos Renieris
```

modules.name: pygraphviz
documentation:
PyGraphviz
==========

A Python wrapper for the graphviz Agraph data structure.

See https://pygraphviz.github.io for complete documentation.
See pygraphviz.AGraph for detailed documentation.

functions.name: pygraphviz.scraper._get_sg_image_scraper
documentation:
Return the callable scraper to be used by Sphinx-Gallery.

It allows PyGraphviz users to just use strings as they already can for
'matplotlib' and 'mayavi'. Details on this implementation can be found in
[sphinx-gallery/sphinx-gallery/494]

[sphinx-gallery/sphinx-gallery/494]: https://github.com/sphinx-gallery/sphinx-gallery/pull/494

classes.name: pygraphviz.scraper.PNGScraper

methods.name: pygraphviz.scraper.PNGScraper.__call__
documentation:
Scrape .png images created by pygraphviz examples.

Invoked by sphinx-gallery.

# Parameters

block

: A tuple containing the (label, content, line_number of the
  block.

block_vars

: Dict of block variables

gallery_conf

: Mapping of sphinx-gallery configuration values

# Returns

str :

: rST-formatted string containing the generated images. This will
  be rendered to HTML during the `sphinx-build` process.

A callable image scraper for png outputs from pygraphviz examples
for sphinx-gallery.

Can be used to collect `.png` files generated by pygraphviz in
sphinx-gallery examples by adding the following to the `conf.py`:

```
sphinx_gallery_conf = {
    ...
    'image_scrapers': ('matplotlib', 'pygraphviz'),
}
```

This class is based on the recipe provide in [^footnote-1].

# References

[^footnote-1]: sphinx-gallery documentation - custom image scraper.
    <https://sphinx-gallery.github.io/stable/advanced.html>

modules.name: examples.plot_miles
documentation:
# Knuth Miles

An example that shows how to add your own positions to nodes
and have graphviz "neato" position the edges.

miles_graph() returns an undirected graph over the 128 US cities from
the datafile miles_dat.txt.

This example is described in Section 1.1 in Knuth's book [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2].

The data used in this example is copied from [^footnote-2].  The filename and
header have been modified to adhere to the request of the author to
not corrupt the original source file content and name.

## References.

[^footnote-1]: Donald E. Knuth,
    "The Stanford GraphBase: A Platform for Combinatorial Computing",
    ACM Press, New York, 1993.

[^footnote-2]: <http://www-cs-faculty.stanford.edu/~knuth/sgb.html>

functions.name: examples.plot_miles.miles_graph
documentation:
Return a graph from the data in miles_dat.txt.

Edges are made between cities that are less then 300 miles apart.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.__del__
documentation: not implemented, but could be...
def __setitem__(self,u,v):
self.add_edge(u,v)

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_node
documentation:
Add a single node n.

If n is not a string, conversion to a string will be attempted.
String conversion will work if n has valid string representation
(try str(n) if you are unsure).

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_node("a")
>>> G.nodes()
['a']
>>> G.add_node(1)  # will be converted to a string
>>> G.nodes()
['a', '1']
```

Attributes can be added to nodes on creation or updated after creation
(attribute values must be strings)

```pycon
>>> G.add_node(2, color="red")
```

See <http://www.graphviz.org/doc/info/attrs.html>
for a list of attributes.

Anonymous Graphviz nodes are currently not implemented.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_nodes_from
documentation:
Add nodes from a container nbunch.

nbunch can be any iterable container such as a list or dictionary

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> nlist = ["a", "b", 1, "spam"]
>>> G.add_nodes_from(nlist)
>>> sorted(G.nodes())
['1', 'a', 'b', 'spam']
```

Attributes can be added to nodes on creation or updated after creation

```pycon
>>> G.add_nodes_from(nlist, color="red")  # set all nodes in nlist red
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.remove_node
documentation:
Remove the single node n.

Attempting to remove a node that isn't in the graph will produce
an error.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_node("a")
>>> G.remove_node("a")
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.remove_nodes_from
documentation:
Remove nodes from a container nbunch.

nbunch can be any iterable container such as a list or dictionary

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> nlist = ["a", "b", 1, "spam"]
>>> G.add_nodes_from(nlist)
>>> G.remove_nodes_from(nlist)
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.nodes_iter
documentation:
Return an iterator over all the nodes in the graph.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
the nodes may produce unpredictable results.  Use nodes()
as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.nodes
documentation: Return a list of all nodes in the graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.number_of_nodes
documentation: Return the number of nodes in the graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.order
documentation: Return the number of nodes in the graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.has_node
documentation:
Return True if n is in the graph or False if not.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_node("a")
>>> G.has_node("a")
True
>>> "a" in G  # same as G.has_node('a')
True
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.get_node
documentation:
Return a node object (Node) corresponding to node n.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_node("a")
>>> node = G.get_node("a")
>>> print(node)
a
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_edge
documentation:
Add a single edge between nodes u and v.

If the nodes u and v are not in the graph they will added.

If u and v are not strings, conversion to a string will be attempted.
String conversion will work if u and v have valid string representation
(try str(u) if you are unsure).

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
>>> G.edges()
[('a', 'b')]
```

The optional key argument allows assignment of a key to the
edge.  This is especially useful to distinguish between
parallel edges in multi-edge graphs (strict=False).

```pycon
>>> G = pgv.AGraph(strict=False)
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b", "first")
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b", "second")
>>> sorted(G.edges(keys=True))
[('a', 'b', 'first'), ('a', 'b', 'second')]
```

Attributes can be added when edges are created or updated after creation

```pycon
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b", color="green")
```

Attributes must be valid strings.

See <http://www.graphviz.org/doc/info/attrs.html>
for a list of attributes.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_edges_from
documentation:
Add nodes to graph from a container ebunch.

ebunch is a container of edges such as a list or dictionary.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> elist = [("a", "b"), ("b", "c")]
>>> G.add_edges_from(elist)
```

Attributes can be added when edges are created or updated after creation

```pycon
>>> G.add_edges_from(elist, color="green")
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.get_edge
documentation:
Return an edge object (Edge) corresponding to edge (u,v).

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
>>> edge = G.get_edge("a", "b")
>>> print(edge)
('a', 'b')
```

With optional key argument will only get edge matching (u,v,key).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.remove_edge
documentation:
Remove edge between nodes u and v from the graph.

With optional key argument will only remove an edge
matching (u,v,key).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.remove_edges_from
documentation: Remove edges from ebunch (a container of edges).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.has_edge
documentation:
Return True an edge u-v is in the graph or False if not.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
>>> G.has_edge("a", "b")
True
```

Optional key argument will restrict match to edges (u,v,key).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.edges
documentation:
Return list of edges in the graph.

If the optional nbunch (container of nodes) only edges
adjacent to nodes in nbunch will be returned.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
>>> G.add_edge("c", "d")
>>> print(sorted(G.edges()))
[('a', 'b'), ('c', 'd')]
>>> print(G.edges("a"))
[('a', 'b')]
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.has_neighbor
documentation:
Return True if u has an edge to v or False if not.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
>>> G.has_neighbor("a", "b")
True
```

Optional key argument will only find edges (u,v,key).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.neighbors_iter
documentation:
Return iterator over the nodes attached to n.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
node neighbors may produce unpredictable results.  Use neighbors()
as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.neighbors
documentation: Return a list of the nodes attached to n.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.out_edges_iter
documentation:
Return iterator over out edges in the graph.

If the optional nbunch (container of nodes) only out edges
adjacent to nodes in nbunch will be returned.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
edges may produce unpredictable results.  Use out_edges()
as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.in_edges_iter
documentation:
Return iterator over out edges in the graph.

If the optional nbunch (container of nodes) only out edges
adjacent to nodes in nbunch will be returned.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
edges may produce unpredictable results.  Use in_edges()
as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.edges_iter
documentation:
Return iterator over edges in the graph.

If the optional nbunch (container of nodes) only edges
adjacent to nodes in nbunch will be returned.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
edges may produce unpredictable results.  Use edges()
as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.out_edges
documentation:
Return list of out edges in the graph.

If the optional nbunch (container of nodes) only out edges
adjacent to nodes in nbunch will be returned.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.in_edges
documentation:
Return list of in edges in the graph.
If the optional nbunch (container of nodes) only in edges
adjacent to nodes in nbunch will be returned.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.predecessors_iter
documentation:
Return iterator over predecessor nodes of n.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
node predecessors may produce unpredictable results.  Use
predecessors() as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.successors_iter
documentation:
Return iterator over successor nodes of n.

Note: modifying the graph structure while iterating over
node successors may produce unpredictable results.  Use
successors() as an alternative.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.successors
documentation: Return list of successor nodes of n.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.predecessors
documentation: Return list of predecessor nodes of n.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.degree_iter
documentation:
Return an iterator over the degree of the nodes given in
nbunch container.

Returns pairs of (node,degree).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.in_degree_iter
documentation:
Return an iterator over the in-degree of the nodes given in
nbunch container.

Returns pairs of (node,degree).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.out_degree_iter
documentation:
Return an iterator over the out-degree of the nodes given in
nbunch container.

Returns pairs of (node,degree).

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.out_degree
documentation:
Return the out-degree of nodes given in nbunch container.

Using optional with_labels=True returns a dictionary
keyed by node with value set to the degree.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.in_degree
documentation:
Return the in-degree of nodes given in nbunch container.

Using optional with_labels=True returns a dictionary
keyed by node with value set to the degree.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.reverse
documentation: Return copy of directed graph with edge directions reversed.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.degree
documentation:
Return the degree of nodes given in nbunch container.

Using optional with_labels=True returns a dictionary
keyed by node with value set to the degree.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.number_of_edges
documentation: Return the number of edges in the graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.clear
documentation: Remove all nodes, edges, and attributes from the graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.copy
documentation:
Return a copy of the graph.

Notes
=====
Versions <=1.6 made a copy by writing and the reading a dot string.
This version loads a new graph with nodes, edges and attributes.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_path
documentation: Add the path of nodes given in nlist.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_cycle
documentation: Add the cycle of nodes given in nlist.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.add_subgraph
documentation: Return subgraph induced by nodes in nbunch.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.remove_subgraph
documentation: Remove subgraph with given name.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.subgraph_parent
documentation: Return parent graph of subgraph or None if graph is root graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.subgraph_root
documentation: Return root graph of subgraph or None if graph is root graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.get_subgraph
documentation:
Return existing subgraph with specified name or None if it
doesn't exist.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.subgraphs_iter
documentation: Iterator over subgraphs.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.subgraphs
documentation: Return a list of all subgraphs in the graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.is_strict
documentation:
Return True if graph is strict or False if not.

Strict graphs do not allow parallel edges or self loops.
directed, undirected tests and conversions

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.is_directed
documentation: Return True if graph is directed or False if not.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.is_undirected
documentation: Return True if graph is undirected or False if not.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.to_undirected
documentation: Return undirected copy of graph.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.to_directed
documentation:
Return directed copy of graph.

Each undirected edge u-v is represented as two directed
edges u->v and v->u.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.read
documentation:
Read graph from dot format file on path.

path can be a file name or file handle

use:

```
G.read("file.dot")
```
io

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.write
documentation:
Write graph in dot format to file on path.

path can be a file name or file handle

use:

```
G.write("file.dot")
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.string_nop
documentation: Return a string (unicode) representation of graph in dot format.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.to_string
documentation:
Return a string representation of graph in dot format.

`to_string()` uses "agwrite" to produce "dot" format w/o rendering.
The function `string_nop()` layouts with "nop" and renders to "dot".

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.string
documentation: Return a string (unicode) representation of graph in dot format.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.from_string
documentation:
Load a graph from a string in dot format.

Overwrites any existing graph.

To make a new graph from a string use

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> s = "digraph {1 -> 2}"
>>> A = pgv.AGraph()
>>> t = A.from_string(s)
>>> A = pgv.AGraph(string=s)  # specify s is a string
>>> A = pgv.AGraph(s)  # s assumed to be a string during initialization
```

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph._run_prog
documentation:
Apply graphviz program to graph and return the result as a string.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> A = pgv.AGraph()
>>> s = A._run_prog()  # doctest: +SKIP
>>> s = A._run_prog(prog="acyclic")  # doctest: +SKIP
```

Use keyword args to add additional arguments to graphviz programs.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.unflatten
documentation:
Adjust directed graphs to improve layout aspect ratio.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> A = pgv.AGraph()
>>> A_unflattened = A.unflatten("-f -l 3")
>>> A.unflatten("-f -l 1").layout()
```

Use keyword args to add additional arguments to graphviz programs.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.tred
documentation:
Transitive reduction of graph.  Modifies existing graph.

To create a new graph use

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> A = pgv.AGraph(directed=True)
>>> B = A.tred(copy=True)  # doctest: +SKIP
```

See the graphviz "tred" program for details of the algorithm.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.acyclic
documentation:
Reverse sufficient edges in digraph to make graph acyclic.
Modifies existing graph.

To create a new graph use

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> A = pgv.AGraph(directed=True)
>>> B = A.acyclic(copy=True)  # doctest: +SKIP
```

See the graphviz "acyclic" program for details of the algorithm.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.layout
documentation:
Assign positions to nodes in graph.

Optional prog=\['neato'|'dot'|'twopi'|'circo'|'fdp'|'nop'\]
will use specified graphviz layout method.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> A = pgv.AGraph()
>>> A.add_edge(1, 2)
>>> A.layout()
>>> A.layout(prog="neato", args="-Nshape=box -Efontsize=8")
```

Use keyword args to add additional arguments to graphviz programs.

The layout might take a long time on large graphs.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph._layout
documentation:
Assign positions to nodes in graph.

:::{caution}
EXPERIMENTAL
:::

This version of the layout command uses libgvc for layout instead
of command line GraphViz tools like in versions \<1.6 and the default.

Optional prog=\['neato'|'dot'|'twopi'|'circo'|'fdp'|'nop'\]
will use specified graphviz layout method.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> A = pgv.AGraph()
>>> A.add_edge(1, 2)
>>> A.layout()
>>> A.layout(prog="neato", args="-Nshape=box -Efontsize=8")
```

Use keyword args to add additional arguments to graphviz programs.

The layout might take a long time on large graphs.

Note: attaching positions in the AGraph usually doesn't affect the
next rendering. The positions are recomputed. But if you use prog="nop"
when rendering, it will take node positions from the AGraph attributes.
If you use prog="nop2" it will take node and edge positions from the
AGraph when rendering.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph.draw
documentation:
Output graph to path in specified format.

An attempt will be made to guess the output format based on the file
extension of `path`.  If that fails, then the `format` parameter will
be used.

Note, if `path` is a file object returned by a call to os.fdopen(),
then the method for discovering the format will not work.  In such
cases, one should explicitly set the `format` parameter; otherwise, it
will default to 'dot'.

If path is None, the result is returned as a Bytes object.

Formats (not all may be available on every system depending on
how Graphviz was built)

> 'canon', 'cmap', 'cmapx', 'cmapx_np', 'dia', 'dot',
> 'fig', 'gd', 'gd2', 'gif', 'hpgl', 'imap', 'imap_np',
> 'ismap', 'jpe', 'jpeg', 'jpg', 'mif', 'mp', 'pcl', 'pdf',
> 'pic', 'plain', 'plain-ext', 'png', 'ps', 'ps2', 'svg',
> 'svgz', 'vml', 'vmlz', 'vrml', 'vtx', 'wbmp', 'xdot', 'xlib'

If prog is not specified and the graph has positions
(see layout()) then no additional graph positioning will
be performed.

Optional prog=\['neato'|'dot'|'twopi'|'circo'|'fdp'|'nop'\]
will use specified graphviz layout method.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edges_from([(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 0), (2, 3)])
>>> G.layout()
```

\# use current node positions, output pdf in 'file.pdf'
\>>> G.draw("file.pdf")

\# use dot to position, output png in 'file'
\>>> G.draw("file", format="png", prog="dot")

\# use keyword 'args' to pass additional arguments to graphviz
\>>> G.draw("test.pdf", prog="twopi", args="-Gepsilon=1")
\>>> G.draw("test2.pdf", args="-Nshape=box -Edir=forward -Ecolor=red ")

The layout might take a long time on large graphs.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph._draw
documentation:
Output graph to path in specified format.

:::{caution}
EXPERIMENTAL
:::

This version of the draw command uses libgvc for drawing instead
of command line GraphViz tools like in versions \<1.6 and the default.

An attempt will be made to guess the output format based on the file
extension of `path`.  If that fails, then the `format` parameter will
be used.

Note, if `path` is a file object returned by a call to os.fdopen(),
then the method for discovering the format will not work.  In such
cases, one should explicitly set the `format` parameter; otherwise, it
will default to 'dot'.

If path is None, the result is returned as a Bytes object.

Formats (not all may be available on every system depending on
how Graphviz was built)

> 'canon', 'cmap', 'cmapx', 'cmapx_np', 'dia', 'dot',
> 'fig', 'gd', 'gd2', 'gif', 'hpgl', 'imap', 'imap_np',
> 'ismap', 'jpe', 'jpeg', 'jpg', 'mif', 'mp', 'pcl', 'pdf',
> 'pic', 'plain', 'plain-ext', 'png', 'ps', 'ps2', 'svg',
> 'svgz', 'vml', 'vmlz', 'vrml', 'vtx', 'wbmp', 'xdot', 'xlib'

If prog is not specified and the graph has positions
(see layout()) then no additional graph positioning will
be performed.

Optional prog=\['neato'|'dot'|'twopi'|'circo'|'fdp'|'nop'\]
will use specified graphviz layout method.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edges_from([(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 0), (2, 3)])
>>> G.layout()
```

\# use current node positions, output pdf in 'file.pdf'
\>>> G.draw("file.pdf")

\# use dot to position, output png in 'file'
\>>> G.draw("file", format="png", prog="dot")

\# use keyword 'args' to pass additional arguments to graphviz
\>>> G.draw("test.pdf", prog="twopi", args="-Gepsilon=1")
\>>> G.draw("test2.pdf", args="-Nshape=box -Edir=forward -Ecolor=red ")

The layout might take a long time on large graphs.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph._manually_parse_args
documentation: Experimental code to parse args relevant for libgvc drawing and layout
some private helper functions

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph._get_fh
documentation:
Return a file handle for given path.

Path can be a string, pathlib.Path, or a file handle.
Attempt to uncompress/compress files ending in '.gz' and '.bz2'.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.AGraph._which
documentation: Searches for name in exec path and returns full path

Class for Graphviz agraph type.

Example use

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G = pgv.AGraph(directed=True)
>>> G = pgv.AGraph("file.dot")  # doctest: +SKIP
```

Graphviz graph keyword parameters are processed so you may add
them like

```pycon
>>> G = pgv.AGraph(landscape="true", ranksep="0.1")
```

or alternatively

```pycon
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.graph_attr.update(landscape="true", ranksep="0.1")
```

and

```pycon
>>> G.node_attr.update(color="red")
>>> G.edge_attr.update(len="2.0", color="blue")
```

See <http://www.graphviz.org/doc/info/attrs.html>
for a list of attributes.

Keyword parameters:

thing is a generic input type (filename, string, handle to pointer,
dictionary of dictionaries).  An attempt is made to automaticaly
detect the type so you may write for example:

```pycon
>>> d = {"1": {"2": None}, "2": {"1": None, "3": None}, "3": {"2": None}}
>>> A = pgv.AGraph(d)
>>> s = A.to_string()
>>> B = pgv.AGraph(s)
>>> h = B.handle
>>> C = pgv.AGraph(h)
```

Parameters:

```
name:    Name for the graph

strict: True|False (True for simple graphs)

directed: True|False

data: Dictionary of dictionaries or dictionary of lists
representing nodes or edges to load into initial graph

string:  String containing a dot format graph

handle:  Swig pointer to an agraph_t data structure
```

classes.name: pygraphviz.agraph.Node

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.Node.get_handle
documentation: Return pointer to graphviz node object.

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.Node.get_name
documentation: handle=property(get_handle)

Node object based on unicode.

If G is a graph

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
```

then

```pycon
>>> G.add_node(1)
```

will create a node object labeled by the string "1".

To get the object use

```pycon
>>> node = pgv.Node(G, 1)
```

or
\>>> node = G.get_node(1)

The node object is derived from a string and can be manipulated as such.

Each node has attributes that can be directly accessed through
the attr dictionary:

```pycon
>>> node.attr["color"] = "red"
```

classes.name: pygraphviz.agraph.Edge
documentation:
Edge object based on tuple.

If G is a graph

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
```

then

```pycon
>>> G.add_edge(1, 2)
```

will add the edge 1-2 to the graph.

```pycon
>>> edge = pgv.Edge(G, 1, 2)
```

or
\>>> edge = G.get_edge(1, 2)

will get the edge object.

An optional key can be used

```pycon
>>> G.add_edge(2, 3, "spam")
>>> edge = pgv.Edge(G, 2, 3, "spam")
```

The edge is represented as a tuple (u,v) or (u,v,key)
and can be manipulated as such.

Each edge has attributes that can be directly accessed through
the attr dictionary:

```pycon
>>> edge.attr["color"] = "red"
```

classes.name: pygraphviz.agraph.Attribute

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.Attribute.__init__
documentation: use for graph, node, and edge default attributes
atype:graph=0, node=1,edge=3

Default attributes for graphs.

Assigned on initialization of AGraph class.
and manipulated through the class data.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()  # initialize, G.graph_attr, G.node_attr, G.edge_attr
>>> G.graph_attr["splines"] = "true"
>>> G.node_attr["shape"] = "circle"
>>> G.edge_attr["color"] = "red"
```

See
<http://graphviz.org/doc/info/attrs.html>
for a list of all attributes.

classes.name: pygraphviz.agraph.ItemAttribute

methods.name: pygraphviz.agraph.ItemAttribute.__init__
documentation: use for individual item attributes - either a node or an edge
graphs and default node and edge attributes use Attribute

Attributes for individual nodes and edges.

Assigned on initialization of Node or Edge classes
and manipulated through the class data.

```pycon
>>> import pygraphviz as pgv
>>> G = pgv.AGraph()
>>> G.add_edge("a", "b")
>>> n = pgv.Node(G, "a")
>>> n.attr["shape"] = "circle"
>>> e = pgv.Edge(G, "a", "b")
>>> e.attr["color"] = "red"
```

See
<http://graphviz.org/doc/info/attrs.html>
for a list of all attributes.
